coredata.dc:title: Synthesis, structural, optical, dielectric, magnetic and magneto-dielectric properties of CoFe2O4 and Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) decorated CoFe2O4 hybrid nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Biswal, Rutam
Yadav, Preeti
Mishra, Pragya
Kumar, Pushpendra
Singh, Manoj K.
coredata.dc:description: A sol-gel synthesis method was used for the synthesis of pure CoFe2O4 (CFO) as well as Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) decorated CoFe2O4 Hybrid Nanocomposite (GCHN). From X-ray diffraction (XRD) data it was analyzed that both these samples have a cubic structure with Fd-3m space group. Using Debye-Scherer's equation, the average sizes were determined to be 24.48 and 13.74 nm, respectively. FTIR spectra revealed details about vibration bands and functional groups present. Raman spectra confirmed existence of GQDs in GCHN with an ID/IG ratio of ∼0.8. The results of Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) analysis exhibited microstructural morphological details having shapes of grains to be non-uniform in shape. UV–visible data was used to calculate band gap (Eg) energy by using Tauc plot method and found to be 2.52 eV and 2.99 eV respectively for CFO and GCHN. Urbach energy (EU) was found to be 1.03 and 1.19 eV for CFO and GCHN respectively. The dielectric properties of GCHN were analyzed for dependence on frequency and temperature and explained by Maxwell-Wagner-type polarization. The frequency-dependent ac conductivity (σac) followed Jonscher's power law and investigated dynamics of ion hopping. Impedance spectroscopy was used to further evaluate Nyquist plots with respect to temperature as well as magnetic fields to estimate grain and grain boundary contributions. This was further used to evaluate electrical characteristics such as relaxation time (τ). Activation energies (Ea) calculated using relaxation times were found to be 0.48 eV for CFO while 0.22 and 0.92 eV for GCHN respectively. At room temperature, CFO and GCHN exhibited a strong magneto-dielectric coupling (MD) and showed negative magneto-dielectric properties in low-frequency regions. At room temperature (RT), magnetization values of CFO and GCHN displayed were 38.48 and 7.52 emu/g respectively. The lower magnetization value was possibly due to shielding by GQDs in GCHN. These properties enable it to be used as a potential application in microelectronic systems, spintronics, optical and optoelectronic devices, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and memory devices.

head.ce:title: Synthesis, structural, optical, dielectric, magnetic and magneto-dielectric properties of CoFe2O4 and Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) decorated CoFe2O4 hybrid nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre of Material Sciences, University of Allahabad, Prayagraj, 211002, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre of Material Sciences, University of Allahabad, Prayagraj,-211002, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Manipal University Jaipur, Jaipur, 303007, Rajasthan, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Manipal University Jaipur, Jaipur 303007, Rajasthan, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sol-gel synthesis method was used for the synthesis of pure CoFe2O4 (CFO) as well as Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) decorated CoFe2O4 Hybrid Nanocomposite (GCHN). From X-ray diffraction (XRD) data it was analyzed that both these samples have a cubic structure with Fd-3m space group. Using Debye-Scherer's equation, the average sizes were determined to be 24.48 and 13.74 nm, respectively. FTIR spectra revealed details about vibration bands and functional groups present. Raman spectra confirmed existence of GQDs in GCHN with an ID/IG ratio of ∼0.8. The results of Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) analysis exhibited microstructural morphological details having shapes of grains to be non-uniform in shape. UV–visible data was used to calculate band gap (Eg) energy by using Tauc plot method and found to be 2.52 eV and 2.99 eV respectively for CFO and GCHN. Urbach energy (EU) was found to be 1.03 and 1.19 eV for CFO and GCHN respectively. The dielectric properties of GCHN were analyzed for dependence on frequency and temperature and explained by Maxwell-Wagner-type polarization. The frequency-dependent ac conductivity (σac) followed Jonscher's power law and investigated dynamics of ion hopping. Impedance spectroscopy was used to further evaluate Nyquist plots with respect to temperature as well as magnetic fields to estimate grain and grain boundary contributions. This was further used to evaluate electrical characteristics such as relaxation time (τ). Activation energies (Ea) calculated using relaxation times were found to be 0.48 eV for CFO while 0.22 and 0.92 eV for GCHN respectively. At room temperature, CFO and GCHN exhibited a strong magneto-dielectric coupling (MD) and showed negative magneto-dielectric properties in low-frequency regions. At room temperature (RT), magnetization values of CFO and GCHN displayed were 38.48 and 7.52 emu/g respectively. The lower magnetization value was possibly due to shielding by GQDs in GCHN. These properties enable it to be used as a potential application in microelectronic systems, spintronics, optical and optoelectronic devices, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and memory devices.

(a) XRD pattern, (b) Frequency dependence ε at diverse temperatures (300–773 K) [Inset shows temperature dependence ε at various frequencies between 100 Hz and 1 MHz], (c) Magneto-Dielectric (MD %) coupling at diverse magnetic fields varying from 0.0 T to 1.5 T and (d) Nyquist plot of Z′ and Z″ data in between 0.0 T and 1.5 T for GCHN [GQDs/CoFe2O4 Hybrid Nanocomposite].

ce:keyword.ce:text: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) / CoFe2O4 hybrid nanocomposite [GCHN]

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the wide availability of cutting-edge functional materials possessing dielectric properties, numerous classes of materials have been thoroughly investigated and have garnered significant attention due to their intriguing electrical, magnetic and optical properties [1–11]. These materials include metal-organic frameworks, spinel ferrites MFe2O4 [1–4], mixed spinel ferrites such as LiMg2Fe3O7 [5], ceramic ferroelectric materials [6], manganite-based perovskite-type oxides ABMnO3 compounds [7], multiferroics like rare-earth manganates RMnO3 [8], mixed valence manganites, rare earth orthoferrites (RFeO3) [9], and spinel chromite oxides ACr2O4 [10] to name a few. These materials have shown potential in spintronics devices [3], optoelectronic devices [10], magnetoresistive transducers, magnetoelectric sensors, read/write heads in computer memory systems, optomagnetic recording, spin-switching valves, spin reorientation-based modulators, magneto-efficiency to name a few [11]. Spinel ferrites i.e. MFe2O4 (where M = Co, Zn, Ni, Mg, etc.) are acknowledged as leading magnetic materials. They consist of oxides primarily composed of ferric ions (Fe3+), making them notable for their novelty and versatility [12–16].

Spinel ferrites exhibit potential for sophisticated frequency applications, enhanced temperature resistance, improved resistance to deterioration, and cost-effectiveness. Additionally, they showcase intriguing magnetic, electrical, and optical properties [17–19]. CoFe2O4 (CFO) nanoparticles (NPs) are inverse spinel ferrites with cubic closed-pack arrangement [20]. Fe3+ cations are equally dispersed in inverse spinel throughout Td (tetrahedral) and Oh (octahedral) sites, contrary to Co2+ cations, which solely inhabit Oh site. The charge transfer from Td to Oh Fe sites is what is responsible for the occurrence of a net magnetic moment [21–23]. Additionally, these materials prove to be good dielectric materials possessing high dielectric permittivity (ε) and hold promise in various electronic industry applications such as energy storage devices, actuators, sonars, and micromotors [24]. The low conductivity, high electrical resistivity and strong chemical stability of CFO make them particularly attractive candidates in this field. Furthermore, the unique magnetic properties of ferrites make them suitable for magnetic and electric tunable devices as well as magnetic data storage applications [24].

In recent years, a diverse range of organic nanoscale carbon materials, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been extensively developed due to their extraordinary properties which was an interesting material for researchers worldwide [1–3,25]. GQDs have been found useful for the enhancement of the dielectric properties of spinel ferrite nanostructures [3]. Apart from GQDs, many conjugated polymer/inorganic host hybrid composites have been extensively studied, such as polyaniline/MoO3, polyaniline/V2O5, polypyrrol/mordenites, polythiophene/mordenites etc. [26]. This paper confines its investigation to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) doped with cobalt ferrite (CFO). The existence of functional groups; such as hydroxyl (−OH) besides carboxyl (COOH) on peripheral edges of GQD structures may often be used as bonding agents or coating components [1,27–30]. Also, GQDs have been shown to improve ferrites' dielectric characteristics. Hence, these are often used for the decoration of various materials like spinel ferrites like ZnFe2O4, CoFe2O4, NiFe2O4 etc. [3,31–36]. According to Chen et al. [37], dielectric permittivity (ε) of a nanocomposite of GQDs, cobalt ferrite and polyvinylidene fluoride i.e. CFO-GQD-PVDF ternary composite was enhanced than that of CFO-PVDF composites from 60 to 95,000, while tangent loss (tan δ) of CFO-GQD-PVDF is significantly lesser than that of GQD-PVDF composites. Also, as the electronics industry advances towards miniaturization, researchers and engineers are increasingly interested in embedded capacitors, which can replace current discrete capacitors and be widely used in next-generation electronics; these capacitors require dielectric materials with both high ε and low tan δ, making polymer-based dielectric composites an ideal choice [37]. Panda et al. [38] reported dielectric behavior is attributed to Maxwell-Wagner polarization. Zhang et al. [39] discussed the gradual decrease of ε and tan δ with increasing frequency, reaching a constant value at high frequencies due to electron (e−) hopping between ferrous (Fe2+) and ferric ions (Fe3+). Hence, ferrites make a good material to be blended with GQDs. GQDs when combined with other materials in hybrid nanocomposites, can enhance various functionalities for practical applications such as photocatalysts in solar water splitting, solid-state hydrogen storage, nanocomposites with higher electron density interconnected with S-GQDs or N-GQDs with ferrite nanostructures, battery electrodes, removal of heavy metals from contaminated environments, and facilitating photo-degradation of organic dyes and antibiotics [40–42]. The versatile properties of spinel-based organic hybrid materials make them invaluable tools in addressing the pressing challenges associated with water pollution and environmental sustainability as well [24,43–45].

In the investigation of the magnetic properties of spinel ferrites, it was noted that the magnetic behavior of CFO occasionally displays a strong ferrimagnetic nature [44]. Rashid et al. reported the utilization of GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 as a probable material for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agent applications [24]. Naghshbandi et al. observed magnetic hysteresis loops of MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites, demonstrating their ferromagnetic behavior [46]. Tatarchuk et al. reported magnetization field loops of Zn-doped CFO exhibiting a ferromagnetic behavior with a lower hysteresis loop [47]. In their study, Safi et al. [48] presented the magnetic hysteresis (MH) results at RT, revealing that magnetic properties are influenced by size and shape of particles, with the particle size playing a more significant role. Rastgordani et al. observed a decrease in Ms values for CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposites compared to pure CFO possibly due to the presence of GQDs and surface defects of CFO crystallites [29]. While Negi et al. [49] reported a remarkable positive magnetocapacitance response in CFO/Pb1−xSrxTiO3 bilayer films, on the contrary, Garg et al. [50] discussed the negative magnetostriction coefficient of CFO. Selvi et al. [51] have investigated the magneto-dielectric and magneto-electric couplings in CFO–BaTiO3 core–shell nanocomposites. Ravindra et al. [52] have demonstrated that band gap (Eg) of CFO films varies in the order of ∼2.5 with different thin film magnitudes. Holinsworth et al. [53] have revealed that CFO possesses an indirect Eg of 1.2 eV and a direct Eg of 2.7 eV. Based on all such reports it is evident that there is meagre information available on GQDs decorated CoFe2O4 hybrid nanocomposite, and its impact on electrical, optical, magnetic properties, and magneto-dielectric effect is also not well understood. Because they are assembled through coordination bonds between clusters of metal and organic ligands, therefore these hybrid composites have great potential in material design due to their multifunctional properties [33]. However, there is a scarcity of studies investigating the effects of doping graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on spinel ferrites, with a focus on dielectric, magnetic, optical, and magneto-dielectric properties. Hence, the primary objective of this study is to examine the impact of doping graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on pure cobalt ferrite (CFO). The specific goal is to evaluate how a hybrid nanocomposite of cobalt ferrite (CFO) decorated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can enhance dielectric properties, reduce tangent loss values, and potentially enhance other relevant properties.

This paper explores the dielectric, optical, magnetic, and magneto-dielectric characteristics of the GQDs/CoFe2O4 hybrid nanocomposite (GCHN). The sol-gel synthesis method was employed to decorate GQDs onto CFO. Structural analysis confirmed a cubic spinel structure and Raman spectra verified the presence of quantum dots in GCHN. It was observed that due to the decoration of GQDs in GCHN, band gap energy was elevated as compared to CFO. Jonscher's power law described the frequency-dependent ac conductivity (σac). Nyquist plots were plotted using Impedance (Z) spectroscopy with varying temperatures and magnetic fields and electrical characteristics like relaxation time were calculated. Negative magneto-dielectric properties in both dielectric permittivity (ε) and tangent loss (tan δ) were observed with respect to frequency. Additionally, the influence of GQDs on magnetic properties was revealed through the magnetic hysteresis loop of CFO and GCHN at room temperature.

For synthesis, only analytical reagent-grade materials were used throughout the experiment. Cobalt nitrate Co(NO3)2.6H2O (Alfa Aesar), Ferric nitrate Fe(NO3)3.9H2O (Alfa Aesar), Citric acid or CA (C6H8O7), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), distilled water (DW) and ethanol (C2H5OH) was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CFO via sol-gel technique
ce:section.ce:para: In DW, appropriate stoichiometric amounts of 0.2 M Co(NO3)2.6H2O, 0.4 M Fe(NO3)3.9H2O and 0.4 M Citric acid (C6H8O7) were dissolved. The ratio being 1:2:2 for Co(NO3)2.6H2O: Fe(NO3)3.9H2O: C6H8O7. The pH was kept at ∼8. A brown gel was then produced by heating the solution to 80 °C and stirring continuously for 2 h [15,18]. The excess water (H2O) and organic impurities were removed by calcining it at 800 °C for 3 h. The gel gently expands into a smooth fluffy mass as it dries and eventually breaks into brittle-like flakes. Fig. S1 shows synthesis of CFO, Fig. S2 shows mechanism of GQDs synthesis by sol-gel method and Fig. S3 shows the decoration of GQDs over CFO forming GCHN in the supplementary data.

In a beaker, 100 mL (10 mg/mL) NaOH solution along with the initially generated GQD solution was taken. 0.4 M Fe(NO3)3.9H2O and 0.2 M Co(NO3)2.6H2O solutions were shaken vigorously and added into the solution. The pH was stabilized at ∼11 by adding 10 mL NaOH solution. For an hour, this mixture was left at 80 °C to crystallize nanoparticles (NPs). To make a nanocomposite, it was processed at 70 °C and then after temperature was lowered to room temperature (RT) [21,22]. The gel is calcined for 4 h at 800 °C to eliminate excess additional water or organic residues. Fig. 1 depicts synthesis of GCHN using sol-gel methodology.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) explained structural characteristics and phase composition with λ = 1.5402 Å, CuKα operating at 45 kV 40 mA (RIGAKU smart lab diffractometer). For identifying the surface functional groups, the Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) study of the samples was conducted using Spectrum 2 FT-IR spectrophotometer. Samples were prepared with KBr as pellets by creating a homogeneous mixture. Data was recorded as % Transmittance versus Wavenumber (cm−1) within the range of 400–4000 cm−1. Raman microscope equipment was used to generate Raman spectra (Horiba, Inc. LabRAM with Ar+ ion laser source with 514.5 nm λ). Optical features were investigated using (Shimadzu UV-NIR 2600, Japan) Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. Absorbance data was taken at room temperature in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. For this, the powdered samples were dissolved in ethylene glycol and ultrasonicated for 30 min to have uniform dispersion. The uniformly dispersed solution was then poured into the cuvette for obtaining the absorbance data using the Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. The microstructural morphologies were examined using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) equipment (JEOL, JSM-7610FPlus). The elemental compositions were obtained by energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) attached to FESEM. Electrical and dielectric investigations were performed using sample in pellet form. To the pellet was applied conductive silver (Ag) paste followed by drying pellets at 473 K for 2 h. By employing Windeta software, various data parameters pertaining to dielectric studies of the sample with regard to frequency and temperature were collected. The dielectric properties were determined from 100 Hz to 1 MHz range frequency over varying temperature range from 300 K to 773 K using Alpha-A high-performance Frequency Analyzer, NOVO control. For magneto-electric study, Novo Control was deployed up to 1.5 T with variable magnet capacity. Using a (VSM) vibrating sample magnetometer at RT, magnetic properties were evaluated (Quantum Design, USA Model VersaLab 3 T).

Fig. 2 (a) depicts the XRD data of CoFe2O4 (CFO), Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) and GQDs decorated CoFe2O4 Hybrid Nanocomposite (GCHN). The XRD data of CFO and GCHN reveals a clear existence of a spinel phase cubic structure with Fd-3 m space-group. The presence of prominent planes indexed at the diffraction angles (2 θ) in XRD pattern in exact accordance with JCPDS standard cards no. 003–0864 [1] as illustrated in Table S1 of supplementary data. XRD pattern of CFO showed peaks at 2θ = 30.135 (220), 35.495 (311), 37.130 (222), 43.139 (400), 47.233 (331), 53.520 (422), 57.053 (511), 62.652 (440), 65.876 (531), 66.933 (442), 71.081 (620), 74.124 (533) and 75.128 (622). They are in excellent agreement with ferrite structure's peak data for all of the diffraction peaks [1,54–56].

The average size was calculated using Scherrer's equation (eq. (1)):
[1]
τ =
K λ
β
cos
θ
where K is a dimensionless factor (0.94), λ is X-ray wavelength (0.15405 nm) and β is line broadening at FWHM, θ is Bragg's angle (∼17.7 for both) and τ the mean line broadening value.

Using Debye-Scherer's equation (eq. (1)), the average sizes of CFO and GCHN were determined to be 24.48 nm and 13.74 nm, respectively. Hence due to the decoration of GQDs over CFO, the average size of GCHN is reduced as compared to CFO. Moreover, there was good agreement between the notable peaks of GCHN and CFO respectively as shown in Table S1. XRD pattern of GCHN showed peaks at 2θ = 30.108 (220), 35.463 (311), 37.096 (222), 43.099 (400), 47.189 (331), 53.469 (422), 56.998 (511), 62.591 (440), 65.811 (531), 66.866 (442), 71.008 (620), 74.048 (533), 75.050 (622), 79.017 (444). The observation of the diffraction peak of pure GQD (002) is hindered by the presence of CFO crystals that have formed in the gaps between GQDs, resulting in the absence of pure GQDs [22,29]. The lack of the characteristic peak of GQDs could be attributed to a lower quantity and reduced intensity of the (002) peak. The broadness of the diffraction peak suggests a disordered structure (amorphous nature) due to the nanoscale dimensions of the GQDs. The functional groups are predominantly connected to the edges of the GQDs rather than their flat surfaces. It is important to note that the XRD pattern of GQDs does not display a distinct peak, indicating the presence of a disorganized arrangement of atoms within them. The average size of the GQDs was determined to be 6.89 nm. The Williamson and Hall (WH) Plot of CFO and GCHN are illustrated in Supplementary Fig. S4.

Rietveld refinement method (Fig. 3 ) was employed to obtain refinement parameters for CFO and GCHN samples, using a pseudo-Voigt-based function that combines Lorentzian and Gaussian functions [17,19]. The fitness of synthesized samples was determined by calculating χ2 and R factors [57,58] as outlined in Table S2 of the supplementary data. The comparative crystalline size obtained through both Scherrer's and WH methods, along with the strain values, are documented in Supplementary Table S3.

Raman spectroscopy has been employed to examine structural characteristics of carbon-based materials, particularly in case of CFO where Raman data revealed no evidence of signals from impurity phases such as precursor signals [59,60]. The CFO's Raman spectrum illustrated in Fig. 4 (a) demonstrates that oxygen atoms are stretched in a Td (tetrahedral) mode (A1g symmetric stretching) concerning the Fe and Co ions at 692 cm−1, while bands at 473 cm−1 belong to T2g mode for asymmetric stretching as well as bending. The band at 297 cm−1 is ascribed to symmetry-preserving Eg bending of Fe(Co)–O. Moreover, GCHN typically exhibit two bands on their Raman graphs: D (disorder in Csp2) and G (1st-order stretching vibration scattering of Csp2). The distinctive G and D bands are located at 1591.65 cm−1 in addition to 1349.93 cm−1, respectively for GCHN, as shown in Fig. 4(b). Ratio of ID/IG was found to be ∼0.84, demonstrating existence of quantum dots in GCHN [30].

The examination of structural characteristics, morphologies, surface properties, particle sizes, and microstructures for both CFO and GCHN were analyzed by using FESEM images, which are shown in Fig. 5 (a) and 5(b) [24,30]. Both specimens exhibited some sort of clumping due to the interplay of electrostatic forces, magnetic attraction and van der Waals's interactions between NPs [3,61]. It was observed that CFO particles demonstrated a non-uniform distribution of grains [24,62]. The majority of the particles exhibited irregular shapes, including triangular, spherical, and cylindrical. Similarly, the GCHN also displayed irregularly shaped spherical and triangular particles with a non-uniform distribution. The grain size of GCHN was found to be significantly smaller than that of CFO, as evidenced visually [24,63] and by the histogram curves as well; possibly due to a higher tendency to clump compared to CFO particles [63]. The presence of GQDs may inhibit the growth of CFO particles, resulting in smaller particle sizes compared to pure CFO samples. GQDs, being nanoscale materials with high surface area, can influence the surface energy of the nanocomposite, potentially affecting the grain growth behavior of CFO particles. The analysis of morphology shows the presence of small voids due to sample porosity or void fraction and agglomeration in the synthesized materials [64]. Porosity of CFO and GCHN was calculated using Image J software and found to be 19.38% and 12.37% respectively clearly indicating a reduction in the number of pores in GCHN, possibly due to the uniform inclusion of GQDs in CFO [65,66]. The difference in microstructural behavior between CFO and GCHN can be observed from the impedance data as well. CFO possesses only grains and typically lacks substantial grain boundaries as seen from a single RC circuit in impedance spectra obtained for CFO, but the incorporation of GQDs in GCHN introduces additional interfaces between GQDs and CFO grains, resulting in the formation of grain boundaries and interfaces as seen by three component equivalent circuits in impedance data attributed to the interface between the sample and the electrode relaxation (space charge polarization) as the addition of GQDs suppresses induced disorders and inhomogeneity on the grain boundaries/surfaces [67]. Not only the impedance data, even the dielectric data is influenced by the grain boundaries (possessed by GCHN) exhibiting a high dielectric constant [68] for GCHN at 300 K as seen in Fig. S5.

The frequency distribution histogram curve of both CFO and GCHN samples demonstrated a Gaussian distribution, as shown in Fig. 6 obtained using ImageJ software [69,70]. Fig. 6 (a) illustrates the obtained histogram distribution for CFO particles in which distribution ranged from 20 to 160 nm with the average size being ∼ 80 nm. Fig. 6 (b) shows histogram curve for GCHN with the particle frequency of GCHN particles ranging between 10 and 80 nm with the average size being ∼ 45 nm. Therefore, grain size of GCHN was smaller than that of CFO. Similar results were also obtained from crystal structure analysis where the size of GCHN was smaller as compared to CFO.

Line profiling of FESEM data was performed using Gywddion and ImageJ software to analyze the roughness of nanoparticles (NPs), providing insights into their surface characteristics. The Roughness average (Ra) was found to be 489.92 p.m. and 387.78 p.m. for CFO and GCHN respectively. Similarly, Root mean square roughness (Rq) was found to be 634.74 p.m. and 563.01 p.m. for CFO and GCHN respectively. Increased surface roughness can reduce the optical band gap of materials, altering their electronic structure and optical properties due to quantum confinement effects [71]. Rough surfaces lead to increased charge carrier confinement, decreasing the band gap [72]. Surface defects induced by roughness create localized electronic states within the band gap, further reducing its magnitude. In this study, the higher roughness of CFO contributed to a lower band gap. Decreasing surface roughness could potentially increase the electrochemical active surface area and improve the electrode-electrolyte interface influencing the impedance response as observed for GCHN.

Energy Dispersive X-Ray Analysis (EDX) was used for the quantitative elemental composition of materials [1,30]. Fig. 7 (a) and (b) illustrates EDX images of CFO and GCHN respectively. The investigation of CFO and GCHN involved a thorough analysis of element distribution. The findings indicate the existence of specific elements within CFO, namely Co, Fe and O, which account for 14.16%, 32.70 %, and 53.14 % of the composition, respectively. These figures are eloquently presented in Fig. 7 (a), shedding light on the elemental makeup of CFO. Furthermore, the distribution of elements in GCHN was also examined, revealing the presence of C, Co, Fe, and O throughout. Specifically, the elemental compositions of GCHN were determined to be 10.48 % of C, 13.76 % of Co, 32.85 % of Fe and 42.91 % of O as illustrated in Fig. 7(b). This comprehensive analysis provides valuable insights into the elemental constituents of CFO and GCHN, allowing for a deeper understanding of their respective compositions [1].

Fig. 2(b) depicts FTIR spectra of CFO and GCHN. The absorption of Co–Fe is evident through a peak at ∼1020 cm−1, while Fe–O bond is detected at 592 cm−1. The band beneath 600 cm−1 is a unique feature of spinel ferrites. Spinel ferrites' Td and Oh mechanisms vibrate differently, accounting for band position variation [73–75]. Peak at 3373 cm−1 is caused by O–H stretching of the adsorbed H2O. H2O molecule's H–OH bending generates the peak at 1541 cm−1. The wider peak at ∼3416 cm−1 for GCHN is due to O–H bond stretching and indicates nanocomposite's exceptional H2O absorption [30]. The peak formation at 2916 and 1451 cm−1 was caused by C–H stretching [19]. The C–H bond's bending vibration causes the 863 cm−1 peak. A peak at 681 cm−1 was due to Co–O bond vibration and Fe–O stretching vibration at 582 cm−1 confirms the formation of magnetic NPs [57]. All these stretching and bending vibrations present in functional hybrid nanocomposite account for the corresponding peaks.

UV–visible spectroscopy is an effective tool for determining a material's band gap (Eg), as shown in Fig. 8 (a) and (b) through measurements taken of synthesized CFO and GCHN using absorption bands. Due to the valence band to conduction band electrical transition, a remarkably strong optical absorption behavior is observed. The two transitions that have been identified as demonstrating the connection of optical qualities with Beer-Lambert's law by π → π* & n → π* transitions. The surface of GQDs encompasses functionalized O groups, showing sp2 hybridized C=C aromatic bonds via by π → π* transition [73] besides absorption shoulder through n → π* transition of C=O bonds in GCHN [46,52,53]. Therefore, all functional groups of GQDs mimic pure graphitic framework. Tauc plot or Kubelka-Munk function (αhv)2 against photon energy (hv) plot, is illustrated in Figure 8 (a) and (b) (insets), used to compute Eg, with α being absorption coefficient, h is Planck's constant and ν frequency of light. Tauc's relationship is shown in eq. (2) [70].
[2]
(
α h ν
)
2
= A
(
h ν −
E g
)

Eg for CFO and GCHN are 2.52 eV and 2.99 eV, respectively, after extrapolating linear Tauc plot fit [14,76]. From Tauc's Plot obtained band gap corresponds to 2.52 eV (lies within the visible region) and 2.99 eV (close to the visible-UV boundary) both energies correspond to electronic transitions, placing them within the UV–Visible absorption region. These can be hence effectively used as optical devices like optoelectronic devices [10], colorimetric sensors, optical fillers, solar cells and fluorescent labels etc. Incorporating GQDs into CFO results in a reduction in crystallite size, subsequently leading to an increase in the bandgap (Eg) [19]. Increased surface roughness leads to a red-shift (lower Eg) possibly because excited electrons shift from the conduction band to valence band.

The Urbach relation [11,70] provides a method of expressing the dependency of the absorption coefficient (α) [determined by dividing the absorption spectrum and thickness] on energy of photons (hv) in an exponential manner. Urbach energy (EU) which is also known as band tail energy, represents the breadth of localized states in close proximity to conduction energy level, revealing imperfections arising from oxygen and magnetic ions in the stoichiometric and disorder in phonon states [8]. This relationship is given by eq. (3):
[3]
α =
α 0
exp
(
h ν
E U
)
where α0 is constant, hν is photon energy and E
U is Urbach energy. Fig. 9 (a) shows the variation of α with λ for CFO and GCHN. The determination of EU can be achieved by analyzing the slope of the ln(α) vs. hv graph, as depicted in the inset of Fig. 9 (a). Specifically, a linear fitting procedure yields a calculated value of 1.19 and 1.03 eV for CFO and GCHN respectively, which is indicative of the level of disorder in the crystal structure. It is also observed that optical Eg is found to be inversely proportional to EU i.e. CFO possess lower Eg value but higher EU and the reverse is true for GCHN. EU indicates the formation of interbands in between conduction and valence bands. The decrease in band gap values is attributable to the orbital exchange interaction between the band electrons and the localized electrons of CFO and GCHN [66].

Furthermore, Fig. 9 (b) shows transmittance spectra (% T) of CFO and GCHN, which show that transmittance ranges inbetween 5%–35% in the visible part of the bands for CFO and inbetween 1%–30% in the visible part of the bands for GCHN. The high transmittance magnitude confirms the synthesis of a uniform nanomaterial well suited for optical coating applications. The extinction coefficient (k) has been obtained using eq. (4):
[4]
k =
α λ
4 π

Plot of k vs. λ and is illustrated in the inset of Fig. 9 (b). This coefficient quantifies the amount of light loss caused by scattering and absorption within a given volume unit. The high value of k at higher wavelengths signifies that the nanomaterials manifest opacity within this spectrum. The value of k started increasing gradually because of the presence of photons [70]. The location of GCHN's Eg near the UV region allows for the excitation of charge carriers via femtosecond laser pulses, indicating that may have potential applications in ultrafast optoelectronic devices [70].

The refractive index (n) of the material can be approximated as eq. (5) [70]:

where Ts is % transmittance, n is obtained refractive index. n is crucial factor in optical response and in choosing devices for spectral dispersion applications. Moreover, the evolution of this parameter is considerably important for many device applications [11]. Inset of Fig. 9 (c) shows plot of n vs. λ. The resulting plot reveals a significant decrease in n with a gradual increase in λ, indicating normal dispersion nature due to the decrease in absorption and an increase in transmittance. Notably, since the n does not approach zero, some incident photon light is absorbed. The combination of good potential of absorbance, an increment in k with respect to λ, renders GCHN an exceptional material for applications in optical devices [70].

[6]

where δ is skin depth and α is absorption coefficient. Plot of δ vs. λ is illustrated in inset of Fig. 9 (d). δ is found to increase with increasing wavelength for both CFO and GCHN.

The optical Eg of a material is the energy difference between its highest occupied valence band and its lowest unoccupied conduction band determining the minimum energy required to excite an electron (e−) allowing it to conduct electricity. The relationship between the optical Eg and electrical characteristics is primarily through the material's conductivity (σ) which is affected by the accessibility of charge carriers, e− and holes (h+) generated through thermal and optical excitation mechanisms creating an e−- h+ pair.

The optical conductivity (σop) depends strongly on optical parameters such as the frequency of incident photons, the refractive index (n) and the extinction coefficient (k) that is given by eq. (7) [8,11]:
[7]
σ op
=
α
( λ )
n
( λ )
c
4 π k
( λ )
where σop is optical conductivity, c = 2.99 × 108 m s−1 i.e. velocity of light in air and other symbols have similar meanings. Fig. 9 (c) illustrate the behavior of optical conductivity (σop) against wavelength (λ). As shown, the evolution value of σop decreases with the rise of λ. Very high photo response of the samples, may be related to the high absorbent behavior of the material and the excitation of electrons by the incident photons [11]. In addition, a high magnitude value of σop for GCHN was observed compared to CFO, confirming high absorbance and electron excitation.

The electrical conductivity (σele) can be estimated by the optical method by eq. (8) [11]:
[8]

=
2 λ
σ op
( λ )
α

The dependence of σelec on the photon energy is depicted in Fig. 9 (d). The observed results indicate that the electrical conductivity (σelec) exhibits a higher value for GCHN compared to CFO. Understanding σele of a material is crucial for predicting its electrical behavior and light interaction especially in applications such as developing new materials, photovoltaics, light-emitting diodes (LEDs), and semiconductor devices. Tuning optical Eg via doping allows tailoring of electrical properties for driving innovation in electronics, photonics, and renewable energy, shaping future technologies and advancements.

Varying dielectric permittivity (ε) and tangent loss (tan δ) of CFO were measured from 100 Hz to 1 MHz at various temperatures, ranging from 300 K to 773 K as shown in Fig. 10 (a) and (b). Both ε and tan δ decreased monotonically as frequency increased [30]. At higher frequencies, the reduced time available for charge carriers to respond limits polarization processes like electric dipole alignment or charged species reorientation, resulting in lower permittivity. Polarizations (electronic, ionic, and orientational) predominated at lower frequencies, creating interfacial regions that were more apparent at those frequencies. At higher frequencies, space charges had less time to align with the field direction and were unable to experience the relaxation process, resulting in less variation [3,4,76–80]. The low-frequency dispersion was satisfactorily enlightened by Maxwell Wagner-type interfacial polarization [4]. As frequency increases, ε initially decreases before stabilizing due to Maxwell-Wagner model effects, particularly interfacial space charge relaxation. Koop's theory applies to porous ferrite materials, where conductive particles separated by non-conductive boundaries govern dielectric behavior. At lower frequencies, boundary effects dominate polarization, diminishing as frequency rises. Beyond a threshold frequency, polarization stabilizes, as electron hopping cannot match rapid field changes, elucidating the frequency-dependent dielectric response [28].

When ferrous (Fe2+) and ferric (Fe3+) ions inter-exchange e− at Oh site in direction of applied field, e−dislocation occurs [4]. At RT, values of ε and tan δ are located at 37.59 and 0.26 respectively. CFO's high ε and low tan δ can benefit transducers, microwaves, and microelectronics devices. Notably, an extreme tan δ value occurs when the frequency of the peripheral field coincides with the hopping frequency. Similarly, Figure 10 (c) and (d) present fluctuation of ε and tan δ for GCHN between 300 K and 773 K at various frequencies from 100 Hz to 1 MHz. At 300 K, ε and tan δ values are 6.83 × 103 and 32.28 respectively. The trend was almost similar with much higher values of ε and lower tan δ values for GCHN ac compared to CFO. Fig. S5 of supplementary data describes frequency dependence analysis of ε and tan δ values for CFO and GCHN at 300 K (RT). The explanation for high ε and conductivity (σ) lies in exchange interaction of variable oxidation states of Fe and Co ions [38].

Fig. 11 (a) and (b) present temperature-dependent ε and tan δ for CFO at various frequencies. It was observed that as temperature increases beyond 600 K, the value of ε almost remains constant before gradually increasing, whereas the values for tan δ are constant up to 550 K temperature before increasing further. The low-temperature variance observed for both temperature-dependent ε and tan δ near 550–600 K was attributed to the defects, pH conditions during synthesis, and inhomogeneity in prepared sample. The dipole moments transition occurring at high temperatures, i.e. after 600 K, was found to cause the gradual increase in ε and tan δ [37]. But because dipoles are completely incapable of revolving at higher frequencies, fluctuation is insignificant (like for 1 MHz) and variations in orientation follow a similar pattern. Substances with ion-based chemical bonding exhibit minimal temperature variation at frequencies exceeding 100 Hz. Hence, after around ∼650 K, ε does not increase significantly (for higher frequencies) as compared with 100Hz. But due to defects as well as increase in temperature, the shift in the temperature-dependent nature of tan δ plot denotes the magneto-dielectric effect occurring in the sample [4,78]. Figure 11 (c) and (d) shows temperature disparities of ε and tan δ for GCHN from 100 Hz to 1 MHz. As the frequency increases, values of ε as well as tan δ decrease [19]. The value for tan δ increases gradually as temperature rises. As the frequency increases, the variation becomes slightly less noticeable [37].

To comprehend mobility, transition to free carriers and electrical conduction mechanism, AC conductivity (σac) was measured and illustrated in Fig. 12 . Fig. 12(a) and (b) shows frequency dependence σac values from 100 Hz to 1 MHz for CFO and GCHN respectively. The conductivity resulting from free charge carriers in dielectric medium can be explained by two factors: σac and σdc in a single equation. All curves follow Jonscher's power law (with fitting shown by solid red line) as shown in eq. (9) [30]:
[9]
σ ac
=
σ dc
+ A
ω n
where dispersion parameter A denotes polarizability while dimensionless exponent n ranges from 0 to 1 and follows a power law for material's σ. Jonscher's power law states that relaxation phenomenon in σ is frequency-dependent due to mobile charge carriers. The conduction mechanism of Fe2+ ⇌ Fe3+ ions is responsible for variations in slope of power law [38,81,82].

The fitted parameter values for CFO and GCHN are shown in Table 1 , with σdc being almost frequency-independent at higher temperatures and σac being only slightly frequency-dependent at lower temperatures. By using Jonscher's equation, the frequency-dependent conductivity data was effectively fitted, and the resulting parameters shown in Table 1. The observed fluctuations in σdc values with temperature variations, leads to enhanced mobility and subsequent disappearance of H2O molecules within the microstructure of samples. This phenomenon highlights that changes in conductivity are primarily influenced by the absorption of thermal energy by H2O molecules. The σ is due to charge carriers hopping through successful or unsuccessful mechanisms. Successful hopping allows charge carriers to move over long distances and contribute to σdc, while unsuccessful hopping leads to localized movement and a dispersive region. The introduction of GQDs increases the conductivity at 300 K by creating additional pathways among CFO grains, resulting in the formation of two parallel conduction channels in GCHN samples [6]. The behavior of free charge carriers is similar to that of particles confined within a box, with conductivity slightly increasing at lower frequencies [66]. Slight variations observed inbetween CFO and GCHN possibly arises due to contribution by grains and grain boundaries as grains are the regions having high σ, while grain boundaries are the regions having low σ [8]. The electrical conductivity (σ) of a material generally rises with temperature due to increased energy available to charge carriers, lowered activation energy barriers, and reduced phonon scattering, improving electrical conduction. σ heavily depends on the accessibility of charge carriers, such as electrons (e−) and holes (h+), generated via thermal and optical excitation mechanisms. Photon absorption by e-s with energy equal to or exceeding the bandgap (Eg) results in e− - h+ pair creation, affecting conductivity. The surge in frequency induces a remarkable surge in electrical conductivity. The surge in e− migration primarily accounts for the surge in electrical conductivity with frequency [66]. At high angular frequency, the short-range h+ (Co2+–Co3+) and e− (Fe2+–Fe3+) exchange are dominant which contributes to the σ [24]. The surge in σ is likely attributed to the mechanism of conduction involving a variable range-hopping [66]. The results could vary based on the position of the Fermi level, dopant type, and concentration. In the present study, given that CFO possesses a lower bandgap (Eg) than GCHN, it is expected to exhibit higher electrical conductivity due to the easier excitation of electrons. This trend holds true across most temperature ranges, particularly from 323 to 773 K, with the exception being at room temperature (RT). The reason could be the presence of only grains in CFO which enhances σ as reported by Raddaoui et al. [8]. At RT, GCHN shows increased σ, possibly due to the presence of GQDs. Thus, it is possible that at RT, grain and grain boundary contribution is not that effective as compared to the presence of GQDs which plays a dominant role in increasing GCHN's conductivity at RT. A network for percolation was established connecting GQDs and ferrite, in which both entities played a crucial role in enhancing electrical conductivity by reducing particle blocking. This led to an overall increase in conductivity at RT. The transfer of h+ and e− also played a crucial role in facilitating conductivity. These localized states acted as potential wells, allowing carriers to move between wells via a tunnelling mechanism [3]. But as the temperature increases, σ values are found to decrease in GCHN. The presence of grain boundaries could be a possible reason for this reduction. In a nutshell, in most of the temperature range, the relation goes like as Eg decreases, σ increases.

Complex impedance analysis was used to better resolve the transport properties and conductivity relaxation of the materials. It is a vital method for studying the electrical characteristics of substances such as relaxation time (τ), identifying frequency-dependent effects related to heterogeneities, and distinguishing grain and grain boundary contributions [83]. The equivalent electrical circuit representation helps to gain valuable insights into the electrical processes and microstructure of the sample, offering additional understanding [63,64]. Fig. 13 (a) illustrates the frequency-dependence real part (Z′) and Fig. 13 (b) imaginary part (Z″) of impedance at different temperatures of CFO [38]. Similarly, Fig. 13 (c) and (d) demonstrate the same for GCHN. At 773 K the value of Z′ is almost same for all the frequencies while the maximum value of Z′ is seen at RT i.e. 300 K. Similar trend is observed for Z″ also. For GCHN also, similar semicircular trends are observed with higher impedance values as compared to CFO.

As the temperature decreases, the diameter of the semicircular arcs increases, indicating a thermally activated conduction mechanism. The resistance decreases as the temperature increases, suggesting the activation of localized charges. There is a single semicircular arc that represents a specific relaxation process in the charge transport of CFO representing contribution of grains [83,84]. But for GCHN, three combinations of R–C circuits, associating each with the three components of the system: grain, grain boundaries and the electrode are observed. In case of GCHN, the presence of nanoscale grains is expected to enhance ionic conduction by facilitating rapid transport across grain boundaries, thus counteracting the increased impedance caused by larger grain boundary areas in GCHN. The arrangement and impact of curved arcs provide valuable insights into the electrical transport characteristics of the sample, revealing the nature of electrical phenomena and their relationship to the sample's microstructure when represented as an electrical circuit model [83]. Z values at higher frequencies are found to be reduced across the entire temperature range due to a decrease in obstructive characteristics arising from the release of space charge [66]. The primary charge carriers in materials are trapped within grains, grain boundaries, and grain-electrode interfaces. Charge carriers near grain boundaries require higher activation energy, limiting their movement until thermal energy enables hopping. Grain boundaries largely dictate the material's relaxation mechanism, with charge carriers aligning along intergrain barriers. Electrode interfaces have a depletion region containing ions and electrons, requiring more thermal energy for charge liberation compared to grain boundaries. Impedance spectra analysis shows that grain boundaries and electrode interfaces are effective regions for conduction, with barrier layers being the most preferable region for successful ion hopping highlighting the temperature-dependent behavior of charge carriers within materials [38]. The aforementioned phenomenon may also manifest itself when the efficiency of charge transfer processes increases, leading to a decrease in the case of CFO. The occurrence of diminished conduction in CFO can also be attributed to the limited range of charge carriers and the absence of a restoring force caused by constant electric fields [81]. Furthermore, the presence of GQDs and their inherent conductive characteristics can be held accountable for the enhanced conductivity of GCHN [24].

Nyquist plot is represented in Fig. 14 (a) and (b) for CFO and GCHN respectively. The appearance of semicircular arcs with varying patterns indicates swarm of e− process occurring within sample showing how electrical σ and dielectric properties affect micro-structural transformation [20,38]. The Z Simpwin and Z-view software, were used to obtain equivalent circuits involving Nyquist semicircles as shown in Fig. 14. The obtained values of resistances and capacitances obtained for grain (RG, CG), grain boundaries (RGB, CGB), and electrode (RE, CE) for CFO and GCHN obtained by Z-Simpwin and Z-view software are shown in Supplementary Table S4. The RGB values decrease as the temperature increases, suggesting that the prepared sample exhibits somewhat semiconductor behavior [64]. The electrical response of the grain and grain boundary can be explained by two processes: a weaker process from free charge and induced polarization, and a stronger process from the sample-electrode interface; the response may flatten due to nonexponential processes or microstructural nonuniformities resulting in a range of relaxation times [67,83].

Dielectric relaxation time (τ) is the time scale by which dynamic transporters of charge in substances relax or the length of time it takes to become neutralized. Z″ vs frequency plot was used to determine the typical relaxation time using the relation shown by eq. (10) [4]:
[10]
τ =
1 ω
=
1
2 π f
max
where f max is maximum relaxation frequency and other symbols have their conventional meanings as listed above. It was discovered that τ falls off with temperature rise [3,4]. With rising temperatures, semi-circles' diameters shrink, indicating a temperature-dependent process of relaxation. It should be emphasized that more electrons become thermally excited at higher temperatures, significantly shortening the carrier's relaxation time. As a result, the generated dipoles can follow the trajectory of the externally provided alternative electric field owing to the distributed thermal energy. The occurrence of temperature-dependent electrical relaxation events in compositional ferrites is explained by this distinctive mechanism.

Relaxation time (τ) varies with temperature by Arrhenius’ law as shown below in eq. (11) [4]:
[11]
τ =
τ o
exp
(
−
E a
/ kT
)
in which τ0 is pre-exponential factor, Ea is activation energy, k and T are Boltzmann constant and absolute temperature respectively. Fig. 15 (a) and (b) demonstrates plot of ln τ with inverse of temperature for CFO and GCHN respectively. The slope of log τ versus 1000/T curve determined the value of Ea as 0.48 eV for CFO. From the slopes of Fig. 15 (b) and the e values of activation energies for GCHN can be determined. As observed from Fig. 15 (b), two regions, two slopes, and consequently two Ea values were obtained, which were determined to be 0.22 eV and 0.92 eV, respectively [45]. This further clarifies our point that CFO possesses only grain while GCHN possess both grain and grain boundary effects as seen from Fig. 15 respectively. These activation energies prevalent in the material help in thermal activation leading to electron hopping mechanisms and hence conductivity is observed. From the Ea values, it is seen that CFO possess lesser value as compared to GCHN revealing more energy is required to transit e− to higher level in case of GCHN. Similar values are seen in case of Eg values obtained from Tauc plot where CFO shows lower band gap thus showing lesser energy is needed to transit e− in case of CFO. Because e− are easier to transit in case of CFO, hence it shows increased conductivity as compared to GCHN.

Fig. 16 (a) and (b) display how ε and tan δ varies with frequency in different magnetic fields from 0 to 1.5 T for CFO. Similarly, (c) and (d) for GCHN respectively. The substances are categorised as magnetoelectric material if there is coupling between magnetic and electrical order features [30]. The following mathematical formulae can be used to express magneto-dielectric (MD%) and magneto-loss (ML%) couplings quantitatively as shown in equations (12) and (13):
[12]
MD
( % )
=
ε H
−
ε 0
ε 0
× 100
[13]
ML
( % )
=
δ H
−
δ 0
δ 0
× 100
where
ε
H
and
ε 0
are respective ε values and
δ H
a n d
δ 0
are respective tangent loss for applied magnetic field and no magnetic field are found. MD% and ML% values obtained at 1 T for CFO at 1 kHz were found as −13.864 % and −30.051 % respectively. Similarly, for GCHN, obtained MD% and ML% values at 1 T at 1 kHz were found to be −34.017 % and −36.818 % respectively. A negative magneto-dielectric effect is observed, which can be attributed to dipoles disrupting charge order and causing charges to become delocalized, thereby lessening effective polarization [81,82]. This demonstrates that ML and MD% are negative for both samples. Dipoles in external electric field are responsible for undesirable conduct. When a magnetic field is applied, the presence of the magnetic field causes the dipoles within the material to undergo a disturbance in their charge order, thereby resulting in the disruption of the alignment and arrangement of charges. This disturbance in charge order subsequently leads to the phenomenon of charges becoming delocalized, meaning that they are no longer confined to a specific position within the material. As a consequence of this delocalization, the overall polarization of the material is effectively reduced, as the charges are no longer able to maintain a consistent alignment and orientation [83–87]. These properties can be useful in various technological field applications like tunable microwave devices, magnetic sensors, information storage or spintronics devices. Supplementary Table S5 summarizes MD% and ML% values obtained at various fields for CFO and GCHN.

Impedance analysis proved to be a valuable tool in gaining a deeper comprehension of transport properties and conductivity relaxation of materials. In Fig. 17 (a) and (b), frequency-dependent real (Z′) and imaginary (Z″) parts of impedance for CFO and GCHN were depicted at various fields and at room temperature. The findings suggest existence of electrical processes happening within samples, with CFO having lower impedance values compared to GCHN. The appearance of semicircular arcs with varying patterns indicates existence of e− processes within, which is closely linked to effect of electrical σ and dielectric properties on micro-structural transformation [38]. Both Z′ and Z″ exhibit diminishing characteristics as frequency increases, as charge carriers cannot adhere to the high-frequency signal.

In Fig. 17 (a) and (b), it is evident that the Z′ of GCHN has shown an increase when compared to CFO, which is quite intriguing. This phenomenon can be ascribed to significant growth in the number of grain boundaries present in GCHN. As the particle size of GCHN decreases in comparison to CFO, it consequently leads to a noticeable rise in resistivity within the GCHN material. This distinction in resistivity is an intriguing aspect to consider. Moreover, when analyzing the Nyquist plot, it becomes quite evident that the dominant factor contributing to the observed changes is indeed the presence of grain boundaries. Hence, the addition of GQDs increased the impedance values due to its conductive nature. All these findings further support the notion that the increase in grain boundaries within GCHN plays a crucial role in the overall behavior and properties of the material [38].

Fig. 18 shows Nyquist plot of Z′ and Z″ data between 0.0 T and 1.5 T for (a) CFO and (b) GCHN respectively. When Z′ is plotted on abscissa and Z″ on ordinate, a Nyquist plot is obtained. The results indicate electrical processes occurring within samples, with CFO having lower impedance values than GCHN [38]. The Z Simpwin and Z-view softwares were used to obtain equivalent circuits involving Nyquist semicircles as shown in Fig. 18. Supplementary Table S6 presents a summary of resistance and capacitance values obtained for grain (RG, CG), grain boundaries (RGB, CGB), and electrode (RE, CE) for CFO and GCHN by Z-Simpwin and Z-view software. Between semiconducting ceramics and silver electrodes, there is a possibility of Schottky barriers, which RE, CE defines as the contribution of electrode polarization [39]. Q is a persistent phase unit associated with R and C [38]. Here also, CFO shows only grain contribution while GCHN shows grain, grain boundary and electrode contributions. This coupling phenomenon facilitates the development of innovative systems, including magnetoelectric sensors, RF-microwave signal processing devices with voltage-tunable capability, spintronic devices, and magnetic random-access memory devices [70].

The magnetic behavior of CFO and GCHN samples in powdered form was investigated using a vibrating sample magnetometer at 2 T as shown in Fig. 19 . The magnetic hysteresis loop was observed from −20,000 Oe to 20,000 Oe. The loop provided several magnetic parameters such as including coercivity (HC), remanence magnetization (MR), saturation magnetization (MS) and squareness ratio (MR/MS) [80]. CFO and GCHN had MS values of 38.48 emu/g and 7.52 emu/g, respectively indicating decrement in magnetization by addition of GQDs. MR values were obtained to be 15.04 and 1.92 emu/g, MR/MS ratio were 0.39 and 0.25, coercivity was found to be 1395 and 1353 Oe respectively for CFO and GCHN. The magnetic property is determined by the combination of the two antiparallel sublattices, which are connected by strong super-exchange interactions through the O2− ions. This interaction leads to a ferrimagnetic behavior as inferred from figure [44]. The magnetic properties of NPs are greatly affected by both their size and structure, which in turn influence the values of Ms, MR and Hc. It has been observed that as the particle size increases, both the Ms and Hc values also increase, regardless of the crystal structure or particle shape. This suggests that the magnetic behavior of NPs is directly proportional to their size [88], indicating a strong correlation between these two factors. This further clarifies our point of CFO having HC value of 1395 Oe while for GCHN it is 1353 Oe. The magnetic anisotropy for CFO resulted from Co2+ ions' orbital angular momentum being partially quenched [39].

Hysteresis loops may not saturate fully in high applied fields due to insufficient magnetic field strength. Zhang et al. [39] found that increasing sintering temperature raises MS but decreases Hc and MR, indicating sintering temperature's impact on MS due to crystallinity and grain size changes showing variations in grain sizes and crystallinity relating to surface effects. Also, the fluctuation in MS is attributed to changes in cationic movement, notably interchange of Co2+ and Fe3+ ions over Oh and Td sites [22]. The differences in magnetic properties were attributed to presence of non-magnetic layers in GCHN as well as lingering zigzag boundaries passivated by hydroxyl (-OH) assemblages, which significantly reduced the magnetic characteristics within the particles [1,3]. This is caused by GQDs, a high percentage of crystal imperfections, dislodgments, lattice tension inside matrix, and surface defects of CFO crystallites. The reason for reduced Ms values recounts shielding effect of GQDs, surface alteration with canted spin configurations at surface and weakens collinear spin arrangement [1]. Nevertheless, the inclusion of GQDs into GCHN acts as exceptionally promising and potential as a contrast agent in the field of Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) applications and fluorescence imaging [22,24]. Table 2 summarizes a comparison of various obtained parameters of GCHN with a few other similar reported references.

The sol-gel based methodology was successfully used to synthesize CoFe2O4 (CFO) and GQDs/CoFe2O4 Hybrid Nanocomposite (GCHN). The samples have a cubic structure with space group Fd-3m as obtained from XRD data. FTIR revealed details about vibration bands and functional groups. The Raman spectra had an ID/IG intensity ratio of ∼0.8 confirming existence of GQDs in GCHN and the UV–visible showed good optical properties. Band gap (Eg) energy was conveyed using the Tauc plot method and found to be 2.52 eV and 2.99 eV respectively for CFO and GCHN. Urbach energy (EU) was found to be 1.03 and 1.19 eV for CFO and GCHN respectively. The inclusion of GQDs in CFO resulted in average size to decrease, which elevated Eg. FESEM illustrated surface morphology and EDX about elemental composition. Dielectric properties involving ε and tan δ values were analyzed with temperature and frequencies ranging from 300 K to 773 K spanning 100 Hz to 1 MHz. ε slightly decreased with temperature due to dipole thermal vibrations at low temperatures. The ε and tan δ values decreased gradually with growing frequencies and became constant at advanced frequencies due to hopping of e− between Fe2+⇌ Fe3+. Jonscher's power law was obeyed by σ, which is frequency-dependent. The Nyquist plots with varying magnetic fields were shown utilizing Z′ and Z″ data. Activation energies (Ea) calculated using relaxation times were found to be 0.48 eV for CFO while 0.22 and 0.92 eV for GCHN respectively. Negative MD and ML effect was observed for both samples at 300 K. MD% and ML% values obtained at 1 T for CFO at 1 kHz were found as −13.864 and −30.051 %. Similarly, for GCHN it was obtained to be −34.017 and −36.818 %. At RT, CFO and GCHN displayed magnetization value of 38.48 and 7.52 emu/g respectively, because of possible reason of shielding by GQDs in GCHN as well as surface alteration with canted spin configurations at surface leading to decrement. Therefore, all these properties show that GCHN is a potential material for micro-electronics, optoelectronics, magnetoelectric sensors and spintronic devices.
